repo.description: Vim-fork focused on extensibility and usability
repo.name: neovim

body:
## Problem

There are lots of ways to inadvertently introduce security vulnerabilities into GitHub Actions workflows. Currently, this repo doesn't have any automated analysis of CI workflows. Additionally, there are a number of issues with the existing CI workflows that could potentially impact security; while I don't see an obvious way to actually exploit these, best practice is to address these issues proactively.

## Solution

Fix the following issues flagged by Zizmor:

* [Add Dependabot cooldown timer](https://docs.zizmor.sh/audits/#dependabot-cooldown)
* [Pin third-party action dependencies to commit hashes](https://docs.zizmor.sh/audits/#unpinned-uses)
* [Don't persist git credentials on disk](https://docs.zizmor.sh/audits/#artipacked)
* [Set minimal default workflow permissions](https://docs.zizmor.sh/audits/#excessive-permissions)
* [Replace template expansions in code contexts with environment variables](https://docs.zizmor.sh/audits/#template-injection)

There are a few further issues that I marked to be ignored by Zizmor. In particular, the `pull_request_trigger` workflow and the use of `GITHUB_ENV` and `GITHUB_PATH` are common sources of security issues, but as far as I can tell, their usage here seems to be safe. Zizmor also flagged a potential cache poisoning issue that I marked to be ignored since it's only in a test workflow, not anything that produces release artifacts.

comment_data.body:
dangerous use of environment file

[Show more details](https://github.com/neovim/neovim/security/code-scanning/61)
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -3,18 +3,22 @@
 runs:
   using: "composite"
   steps:
-    - run: echo "CACHE_KEY=${{ github.workflow }}" >> $GITHUB_ENV
+    - run: echo "CACHE_KEY=${GITHUB_WORKFLOW}" >> $GITHUB_ENV

@@ -3,18 +3,22 @@
 runs:
   using: "composite"
   steps:
-    - run: echo "CACHE_KEY=${{ github.workflow }}" >> $GITHUB_ENV
+    - run: echo "CACHE_KEY=${GITHUB_WORKFLOW}" >> $GITHUB_ENV

     - if: ${{ matrix }}

comment_data.body: and that emoji needs to go.
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -0,0 +1,24 @@
+name: GitHub Actions Security Analysis with zizmor 🌈

comment_data.body: why? wouldn't that leave you at the mercy of leaving a security flaw exposed for up to a week?
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -6,3 +6,5 @@ updates:
       interval: "daily"
     commit-message:
       prefix: "ci"
+    cooldown:
+      default-days: 7

comment_data.body:
It's meant to mitigate a scenario where an action maintainer's account is compromised and used to push a malicious update; in most cases, such compromised updates are only up for a relatively short period of time (hours or days) before being pulled, and a cooldown timer prevents updates from being pulled in that window. [This blog post](https://blog.yossarian.net/2025/11/21/We-should-all-be-using-dependency-cooldowns) goes into more detail on the case for dependency cooldowns.

While this does also delay receiving security updates for workflows, my understanding is that CI/CD experts still consider it to be a good tradeoff for the large majority of projects.
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -6,3 +6,5 @@ updates:
       interval: "daily"
     commit-message:
       prefix: "ci"
+    cooldown:
+      default-days: 7

comment_data.body: will dependabot auto update this for us?
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -21,7 +26,7 @@ jobs:
 
       - name: Create backport PR
         id: backport
-        uses: korthout/backport-action@v4
+        uses: korthout/backport-action@4aaf0e03a94ff0a619c9a511b61aeb42adea5b02 # v4.2.0

comment_data.body: Yes, dependabot auto-updates hash-pinned action dependencies the same as tag-pinned action dependencies.
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -21,7 +26,7 @@ jobs:

-        uses: korthout/backport-action@v4

@@ -21,7 +26,7 @@ jobs:

-        uses: korthout/backport-action@v4

comment_data.body: Why is `appimage_tag` in lowercase when other environment variables are in uppercase?
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -65,10 +65,17 @@ jobs:
           persist-credentials: false
       - run: ./.github/scripts/install_deps.sh
       - run: sudo apt-get install -y libfuse2
-      - run: echo "CMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=${{ needs.setup.outputs.build_type }}" >> $GITHUB_ENV
+
+      - run: echo "CMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=${CMAKE_BUILD_TYPE}" >> $GITHUB_ENV
+        env:
+          CMAKE_BUILD_TYPE: ${{ needs.setup.outputs.build_type }}
+
       - name: appimage
+        env:
+          appimage_tag: ${{ needs.setup.outputs.appimage_tag }}
         run:  |
-          ./scripts/genappimage.sh ${{ needs.setup.outputs.appimage_tag }}
+          ./scripts/genappimage.sh ${appimage_tag}
+

comment_data.body: Fixed.
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -65,10 +65,17 @@ jobs:
           persist-credentials: false
       - run: ./.github/scripts/install_deps.sh
       - run: sudo apt-get install -y libfuse2
-      - run: echo "CMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=${{ needs.setup.outputs.build_type }}" >> $GITHUB_ENV
+
+      - run: echo "CMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=${CMAKE_BUILD_TYPE}" >> $GITHUB_ENV
+        env:
+          CMAKE_BUILD_TYPE: ${{ needs.setup.outputs.build_type }}
+
       - name: appimage
+        env:
+          appimage_tag: ${{ needs.setup.outputs.appimage_tag }}
         run:  |
-          ./scripts/genappimage.sh ${{ needs.setup.outputs.appimage_tag }}
+          ./scripts/genappimage.sh ${appimage_tag}
+

comment_data.body:
It seems that the backport workflow is now broken. Perhaps this needs to be `true`?
```
Push branch to origin
/usr/bin/git push --set-upstream origin backport-38261-to-release-0.11
fatal: could not read Username for 'https://github.com/': No such device or address
Branch backport-38261-to-release-0.11 may already exist, fetching it instead to recover previous run
/usr/bin/git fetch --depth=1 origin backport-38261-to-release-0.11
fatal: couldn't find remote ref backport-38261-to-release-0.11
Git push to origin failed for release-0.11 with exitcode 128
Create comment: Git push to origin failed for release-0.11 with exitcode 128
```
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -12,6 +15,8 @@ jobs:
     runs-on: ubuntu-latest
     steps:
       - uses: actions/checkout@v6
+        with:
+          persist-credentials: false

comment_data.body:
The nightly tag now points to a wrong commit that doesn't match the commit in the release notes.
```
fatal: could not read Username for 'https://github.com/': No such device or address
```

comment_regular_data.body:
You are seeing this message because GitHub Code Scanning has recently been set up for this repository, or this pull request contains the workflow file for the Code Scanning tool.

### What Enabling Code Scanning Means:

- The 'Security' tab will display more code scanning analysis results (e.g., for the default branch).
- Depending on your configuration and choice of analysis tool, future pull requests will be annotated with code scanning analysis results.
- You will be able to see the analysis results for the pull request's branch on this [overview](/neovim/neovim/security/code-scanning?query=pr%3A38196+is%3Aopen) once the scans have completed and the checks have passed.

For more information about GitHub Code Scanning, check out
[the documentation](https://docs.github.com/code-security/code-scanning/introduction-to-code-scanning/about-code-scanning).

I'm not comfortable adding an unknown external workflow, and there's a number of changes here that look... questionable, all lumped together in a single PR. Automated code scanning is too noisy in general (as evidenced by the (false?) positives flagged here on changes in your own commits).

Zizmor is pretty widely used for static analysis of GitHub Actions workflows, and I've found it to be a very useful tool with an acceptably low rate of false positives. (I personally have it set up as an LSP in Neovim to flag issues with CI workflows as I'm editing them.)

In any case, would you prefer that I open separate PRs for the individual changes? I already split them into separate commits to make it easier to review, so it'd be simple enough to make them into separate PRs.

The issue flagged by the Zizmor workflow above is one of the ones I mentioned in the PR description: that the use of `GITHUB_ENV` is potentially dangerous (though I think it's probably fine here). This issue wasn't introduced by my PR; it was just flagged by the Zizmor workflow because I made changes to the same lines of code (replacing template expansions with environment variable substitutions).

Yes, I think we should take it more slowly. 

1. Can you point to other prominent projects using this workflow?
2. How configurable is it to disable (or, better yet, opt-in to) individual checks?
3. I am against pinning default actions to commits; that will require even more maintenance to manually pull in (security) updates. (We're running on GH infrastructure, so we may as well trust these workflows.)

> Can you point to other prominent projects using this workflow?

* [Python uses it as a pre-commit hook](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/149c4657507d17f78dd0938419a5a24ed71dc07e/.pre-commit-config.yaml#L105)
* [Podman uses the workflow](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/014fd196d5b128f9a65119f25c2ad14c3b176a71/.github/workflows/zizmor.yml)
* [Docker uses it to audit their official workflows](https://github.com/docker/github-builder/blob/e28a7fc7609bba663bacfa94c49da0b120b1b687/.github/workflows/.zizmor.yml)
* [Homebrew calls the CLI tool in a workflow](https://github.com/Homebrew/brew/blob/aa4eedbaf2b7b22bd73aad9e8b00fe77a1bb3341/.github/workflows/actionlint.yml#L50)

> How configurable is it to disable (or, better yet, opt-in to) individual checks?

This can be configured through `.github/zizmor.yml`: https://docs.zizmor.sh/configuration/

Doesn't look like there's a way to set it to be opt-in, but you can certainly opt out of individual checks.

> I am against pinning default actions to commits; that will require even more maintenance to manually pull in (security) updates. (We're running on GH infrastructure, so we may as well trust these workflows.)

That's a fair point; I'll change those back. The `unpinned-uses` audit can be configured to accept pinning to a tag for `actions/*` dependencies: https://docs.zizmor.sh/audits/#unpinned-uses-configuration

Also, after a second look at it, I'm going to get rid of my last commit disabling caching in setup-zig; I don't think there should be any risk of cache poisoning since that workflow doesn't produce artifacts.

Okay, I configured Zizmor to ignore the remaining issues that it had flagged, since the way they're used in the workflows here doesn't seem to be problematic as far as I can tell. So, I think this should be ready for review. (Unless you'd still like me to close this and split it into multiple PRs rather than having several commits in one PR? I could do that too, just let me know.)

Agree with adding the `permissions` and `persist-credentials`; those are clear improvements. Negative on pinning to commits (the security gain does not outweigh the extra maintenance friction _for these_). Lukewarm on more automated code scanning (CodeQL had way too many false positives, and my opinion on bot comment spam on PRs is well-known, and Zizmor is simply an unknown for me).

But not my decision; @dundargoc is the expert here.

@justinmk I can split the addition of the Zizmor workflow into a separate PR, no problem. (I'll have time to do that in an hour or two.)

However, I would strongly advocate for pinning third-party action dependencies by commit hashes. Out of all the changes in this PR, I'd consider this one to be the most important for security.

Taking the backport action as an example, if the maintainer of that action were to have their account compromised or credentials stolen, the "v4" tag could be changed to point to a commit with malicious code, and that would result in malicious code execution in any CI workflows that depend on the action by that tag; in this case, it's used in a workflow that has `contents: write` permissions, so that would be quite bad. (This sort of attack isn't just a hypothetical; [here's a recent example](https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/github-actions-supply-chain-attack/) of a supply-chain attack via GitHub Actions dependencies.)

If the frequency of dependabot PRs is an issue, I think dependabot can be configured to combine multiple action dependency updates into one PR.
Okay, I just removed the commit that adds a Zizmor workflow. I'll make a separate PR for that later as suggested.

looks like the puc-lua CI jobs are consistently failing, even with the commit from 2 days ago

```
CMake Error at CMakeLists.txt:193 (message):
  Failed to find a Lua 5.1-compatible interpreter
```
Thanks again to @HastD and reviewers. Supply chain risk is a very important topic.
